chunk_summary: Stepwise management of feeding aversion in neurodisability involves addressing medical, nutritional, feeding skill, and psychosocial domains, prioritizing safety and pain drivers before implementing structured routines and interdisciplinary support.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of medical pain drivers in feeding aversion and their management.
Learn how to assess and address feeding skill mismatches using IDDSI levels and texture ladders.
Develop strategies to manage psychosocial cycles contributing to feeding refusal.
Recognize when to refer to interdisciplinary feeding teams for persistent issues.
key_facts: Feeding aversion in neurodisability often involves medical, nutritional, feeding skill, and psychosocial domains.
Safety concerns like choking, cyanosis, or recurrent pneumonia require stabilization before therapy.
Constipation is a frequent hidden driver in India; a written bowel plan is often the first intervention.
Use IDDSI levels to rewrite diets and prescribe graded texture ladders with one-variable changes.
Structured routines with fixed meal times and positive reinforcement reduce caregiver distress and feeding refusal.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Feeding aversion in neurodisability is rarely 'just behavioral.' Use the PFD domains (medical, nutritional, feeding skill, psychosocial) to prevent the common error of escalating pressure to eat when the driver is pain or skill mismatch. — Reference 1
document_key_topics: Stepwise management of dysphagia and pain drivers
